bibliographic_data.title: SMOL: Professionally translated parallel data for 115 under-represented languages
bibliographic_data.abstract: We open-source SMOL (Set of Maximal Overall Leverage), a suite of training data to unlock machine translation for low-resource languages. SMOL has been translated into 124 under-resourced languages, including many for which there exist no previous public resources, for a total of 6.1M translated tokens. SMOL comprises two sub-datasets: SMOLSENT and SMOLDOC, each carefully chosen for maximum impact given its size. We demonstrate that using SMOL to prompt or fine-tune Large Language Models yields robust CHRF improvements. In addition to translation, we provide factuality ratings and rationales for all documents in SMOLDOC, yielding the first factuality datasets for most of these languages.

core_content.novelty_claim: SMOL (Set of Maximal Overall Leverage) provides professionally translated sentence- and document-level data for 124 low-resource languages, including many with no previous public resources.
core_content.key_methodology: The dataset comprises two sub-datasets: SMOLSENT, a set of sentences chosen for broad unique token coverage, and SMOLDOC, a document-level resource focusing on a broad topic coverage. The data is used to fine-tune and prompt Large Language Models for translation tasks, with factuality annotations provided.
outcomes: Robust CHRF improvements when using SMOL to fine-tune or prompt LLMs
First factuality datasets for many of the 124 languages
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper introduces SMOL (Set of Maximal Overall Leverage), a dataset designed to unlock machine translation for low-resource languages. SMOL contains two sub-datasets: SMOLSENT, which includes 863 English sentences covering 5.5k common tokens translated into 88 languages, and SMOLDOC, which consists of 584 English documents on various topics, domains, and tokens, professionally translated into 106 languages. The authors demonstrate that using SMOL to fine-tune or prompt Large Language Models yields significant CHRF improvements. Additionally, factuality ratings are provided for all documents in SMOLDOC, creating the first such datasets for many of these languages.
